I want to focus on something I'm actually going to add on to the certification course I'm going to fill with today. So when I kind of get warmed up here, I'll give you the benefit of this information here. It has to do with this discrepancy between the two supposed types of sales models that so-called sales trainers purport to, you know, differentiate. Number one would be something called the old model of selling. And then the other one's the so-called new model of selling. And let me explain what I mean here. The old model, no matter how we say this before, I'm going to go through it deeper here. The old model of selling is that sort of hard charging ABC always be closing sort of model where you spend according to them the first 10% building rapport, next 20% qualifying your customer, the next 30% of the time you spend making a presentation and the 40% large truck is closing the deal, overcoming objections and closing, right? That's the old model that sort of hard charging a type A personality of salesman who doesn't really give about that prospect. He just wants to sell them something, right? That's the old model. The new model is like more of a consultative model, which makes me laugh by the way, this joke, but the consultative model states that what you're doing is you spend the opposite 40% of your time building relationships, 30% of your time identifying needs, 20% of your time offering solutions and 10% of your time confirming closing. In other words, you're only closing 10% of the time. That's according to the consultative model. Those where you spend so much time building rapport, building relationships, doing all this stuff with your prospect that by the time you're done with that, there's very little left to do. What a f***ing joke that is. It's just not true. So what happens is, and here's the discrepancy, both models are f***ing in other words, this whole idea that you're only doing certain things at certain times of a sale is nonsense. You're doing all things at once. In other words, you're always looking to build rapport, strengthen the relationship. You're always looking, you're always in the position in yourself, sharp, enthusiastic expert in your field. Anytime you stop or you fall out of rapport, you're done. You can't close. You can't influence. It's a f***ing must. Same thing goes with asking questions. Certainly at times when you ask dedicated, you ask your questions in a certain order, but you never stop gathering until you're always going to dig deeper, pull back the layers, find out what's really essentially, make sure prospect tick, what's holding them back, what their pain is, what their core need. You never stop asking questions. In terms of your presentation, I'm always pr- my first word out of my mouth is the presentation. I'm the solution. In other words, the idea, I offer a solution 20% of the time, f***ing nonsense. I'm your solution. I'm part of three tens on your solution. Product company, you. So from the moment I open up my mouth, I'm the solution. So I'm always doing that. And lastly, okay, as I said, closing f***ing, okay. If I was closing only 10% of the time as the consultative model calls for, I'd be broke. Conversely, I'm not just closing someone on 40% of the time. I'm always closing the whole ABC model, always be closing. It doesn't mean that you're always asking for the order. What it means is that with every word out of your mouth, every step you take, everything you do, every document, every sales that you use, you hand someone, they're all designed to essentially move the prospect to the next step down the line in the sales process of creating certainty. Bottom line. You're doing all things at once. You never stop asking questions. You never stop building rapport because if you fall out of it, you're done. Well, you break rapport, you're done, right? Number three, my whole presentation from the first word out of my mouth is the solution. And I'm always closing them on something. Whether it's just that I'm the person we're listening to, to answer my questions honestly and forthrightly, I'm always closing them on something. So this nonsense that, you know, there's these distinctions that I spend 10% of my time closing is a passport to failure and misery. Bottom f***ing line. In other words, they make it seem like that you don't have to really ever close much. They really are no objections to overcome because you just spend so much time building rapport, so much time understanding your clients needs that when you finally ask for the order, they just get to say yes. I mean, think about the insanity of that. It doesn't, it's nonsense. So what's happening is a sale, a true straight line sale is both consultative and closing based. They're both, they're all true at all times. As you move someone down the straight line, you're always closing them. If I'm nothing more than listening to your next sentence, and it makes sense for them, the prospect to take time out of their busy day to listen to what you got to say because you're an expert in your field, you could help them resolve their problem, overcome their pain, feel good again, feel back in control. That's what salespeople do. All right. And by the way, if you ever get to the point where you stop asking questions, you're done. You'll fall out of rapport. That's why you notice when we loop, we're always asking questions. You know, I mean, it doesn't make sense to you if I've been always asking them question, what's the worst that can possibly happen? You never stop asking questions. You never stop building rapport and you never stop positioning yourself as an expert and being in control. That's the way it is. So don't get caught up in this nonsense of sales models. There's only one truth about selling, and that is that every sale is the same. Bottom line, every sale is the same because the same five core elements must line up in every sale. They must, number one, love your product. Number two, they must trust and connect with you. Number three, they must trust the company. Number four, if necessary, you must lower their action thresholds. They have a high one. And number five, must add on pain if they're in denial and they're not buying as a result of that. They always have to be like cracking that safe, the safe crackers manual, right? That's what the straight line is. So don't buy into any nonsense or sales or something else. All right. There's nothing wrong with that. It's not the fact that you're always closing them on something. That's a good thing. You're keeping someone interested. You're keeping them in the game, keeping them hanging on your every word. You can't help someone. You can't help someone resolve a need if they're in control. They will bat you around. They'll abuse you. They'll waste your time. I saw it happen my Stratonites back in the day before they went to straight line. They could not close a door because they were not in control. Getting control step one. What happens after that? Separate story. You'll know we know the straight line, but that's your first step.
